title: Regulation: Chalmers taps scandal-linked former KPMG partner for APRA role
full_text:
One of Treasurer Jim Chalmers' picks for deputy chair of the banking and superannuation regulator, David Bradbury, is a former KPMG partner who was involved in one of the firm's contracts under scrutiny in the audit leaks scandal.
Bradbury, a former Labor minister, was instrumental in KPMG's bid for Macquarie's $75 million-a-year audit contract last year, in a process that a whistleblower has alleged shows how the firm abuses conflicted relationships to try to shore up work.
Bradbury only resigned from KPMG's partnership on Tuesday. He is also chair of the Board of Taxation, which advises the government on tax law reform from a business perspective.
Chalmers praised Bradbury's economic policy, regulation and public administration experience on Friday, suggesting he would bring strong leadership and fresh thinking to the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority.
He also nominated APRA member Therese McCarthy Hockey to become deputy chair in an expansion of the board to two deputies. Chalmers said the additional role would help the regulator do its job at a time of increasing scale and complexity in the financial system.
KPMG won the audit tender for Macquarie, the most valuable contract on the ASX, last year. It was the first time in three decades the contract had been put out to tender.
A whistleblower who formerly worked at KPMG alleged the tender process was an example of how KPMG used inside information and personal relationships with potential clients to shore up work, in this case through Macquarie director and audit committee chair Michelle Hinchliffe, a 37-year KPMG veteran.
Bradbury was one of six partners designated as the "core leadership" on the Macquarie pitch, as the team's regulatory lead. AFR Weekend understands he played a key role in the firm securing the contract.
He was involved in pitching the bank directly, and the firm promoted his skills and experience to navigate international regulatory risks as part of its service offering. It is unclear who will now provide this expertise.
Other partners involved in the pitch included recently ousted chief operating officer Eileen Hoggett. One of the whistleblower's main allegations, which has since been substantiated, was that auditors accessed confidential Lendlease documents regarding audit pitches by EY and Deloitte and showed them to partners pitching against these firms at Westpac. Hoggett was one of the partners to view them, as was another unnamed audit director (now partner) involved in the Macquarie pitch.
KPMG's "global lead partner" on the pitch was Rachel Gatt. Hinchliffe met with Gatt and KPMG director Charles Hatchman before their pitches. Macquarie even brought Hatchman in to teach it how to run a tender. Hinchliffe also attended early-stage bid meetings from EY for the tender and her committee then blocked the firm from applying, essentially making KPMG the only viable candidate.
Macquarie has insisted Hinchliffe's conflicts of interest were appropriately managed and stood by the integrity of the tender process. But at least one shareholder is planning to ask questions about its choice of auditor at its upcoming annual meeting on July 23.
There is no suggestion that Bradbury acted inappropriately during the tender process. He was a partner at KPMG for 23 months in its consulting division, with most of his career being in public sector or international roles.
Chalmers also appointed him to chair the Board of Taxation in April, a role he held while still at KPMG and which he will resign from to take up the APRA role.
His appointment there raised eyebrows in the industry, given the board's purpose of giving the government "a business and tax community perspective to improving the design and operation of taxation laws" and Labor's lacklustre efforts to implement reforms to toughen regulation of accounting firms following the PwC tax leaks scandal.
Bradbury joined KPMG in 2024 after a decade in Paris as a senior tax official at the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development, where he worked on international efforts to crack down on profit shifting and tax avoidance by multinationals.
Originally a corporate tax lawyer, Bradbury was assistant treasurer in the Gillard Labor government.
Chalmers said Bradbury and McCarthy Hockey were selected for five-year terms after a merit-based selection process chaired by Treasury secretary Jenny Wilkinson, with APRA chair John Lonsdale and Attorney-General's Department secretary Katherine Jones on the selection panel.
The treasurer's office provided a comment from former Coalition finance minister and current OECD secretary-general Mathias Cormann endorsing Bradbury's appointment.
"He combines deep policy expertise, sound and balanced judgment with real integrity and a real understanding of financial systems. Australia's financial system will be well served by his leadership," Cormann said.
Both appointments require approval from Governor-General Sam Mostyn. If approved, McCarthy Hockey would take up her role next week and oversee regulation of the banking sector. Bradbury would start in September and oversee superannuation regulation.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Hannah Wootton and Edmund Tadros
heading: Regulation
